title: Rugelach
subtitle: Cream cheese pastry rolls with walnut and jam filling
excerpt: Crescent-shaped pastries made from a cream cheese dough rolled thin, spread with apricot jam, chopped walnuts, raisins, and cinnamon sugar, then rolled into tight crescents and baked until golden and flaky. The cream cheese gives the dough a tang and tenderness that shortcrust cannot match. The dough improves with two days in the refrigerator.
sourceNotes: Rugelach are an Ashkenazi Jewish pastry with roots in central and eastern Europe; the name is Yiddish for roughly 'little twists' or 'little rolls'. The cream cheese dough version became the standard through Jewish American bakeries in the twentieth century and was documented extensively in American Jewish community cookbooks from the 1950s and 1960s. The filling of jam, nuts, raisins, and cinnamon sugar is the established formula across published versions of this period. The recipe here follows the American Jewish bakery tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled unbaked rugelach on a tray, then bag once solid. Bake from frozen, adding 5 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles straightforwardly. Keep dough refrigerated between batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dough can be refrigerated for up to three days before rolling. Baked rugelach keep for four days in a tin.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Rugelach can colour unevenly if the oven runs hot; an oven thermometer is worth using.

glossaryTerms.definition: To shape rugelach, a circle of chilled dough is spread with filling then cut into wedges like a pizza. Each wedge is rolled tightly from the wide outer edge toward the point, then curved slightly into a crescent. Because the dough is fat-rich and fragile, it must be cold throughout; if it softens and tears, return it to the fridge for 15 minutes before continuing.

content.text: The richness of rugelach comes from the cream cheese dough, which gives the pastry a tang and tenderness that shortcrust does not have. Divide the dough into four portions immediately after mixing and refrigerate each before rolling.
is the most time-consuming step; it goes quickly once you have the rhythm.

Make the dough by combining the flour, cold cream cheese, cold butter, and salt in a bowl. Rub or cut the fat into the flour until the mixture clumps into a rough dough — do not work it smooth. Divide into four equal portions, press each into a disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for at least 2 hours or overnight.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Mix the walnuts, raisins, brown sugar, and cinnamon together in a bowl. Work one disc of dough at a time, keeping the rest refrigerated. On a lightly floured surface, roll each disc into a circle about 28 cm across and 3 mm thick. Spread a thin layer of apricot jam over the surface, then scatter a quarter of the nut mixture over it, pressing lightly to help it adhere. Cut the circle into 8 wedges. Roll each wedge tightly from the wide edge toward the point, then place on the lined tray, point-side down, and curve the ends slightly inward. Repeat with the remaining discs.

Bake for 20 to 22 minutes until the pastry is deep golden on the underside and pale gold on top, and the filling has bubbled at the edges. Cool on the tray for 5 minutes before moving to a rack — the pastry is fragile while hot.
